spans.name: subagent:GitHub Copilot Chat
spans.input: [{"role":"user","parts":[{"type":"text","content":"I am asking you to check all traces in grafana, why do you look at local?"}]}]
spans.output:
[{"role":"assistant","parts":[{"type":"text","content":"Sounds gucci. I looked at local chat-session artifacts only as a debugging shortcut when the Grafana matches were ambiguous. That was for correlation, not because I think local should be the source of truth for this task.

The actual export path in [scripts/export-tempo-traces.py](scripts/export-tempo-traces.py) reads Grafana Tempo only. It does not read those local chat-session files when generating [trace.json](data/analysis/299224/gpt-5.4-2026-04-11/trace.json) or the per-run `trace-metadata.json` files. The only non-Grafana input it uses is the on-disk `proposed-fix.md` and `validation.md` mtimes to define the search window.

So your point is correct: for “find and capture all traces,” I should rely on Grafana only. The local session files are useful for debugging, but they should not drive the capture logic or the final classification. From here I’ll keep the provenance strict: Grafana payloads are the evidence, and if Grafana does not prove a trace is relevant, I will leave that analysis unfilled rather than backfilling it from local artifacts.

The next fix is to make the exporter do a Grafana-wide crawl of the relevant day windows and build the analysis-to-trace map from Grafana alone, instead of per-analysis lookup plus manual local debugging."}]}]

spans.name: subagent:GitHub Copilot Chat
spans.input: [{"role":"user","parts":[{"type":"text","content":"Yes re-run the full export to find everything we did for the March analysis. "}]}]
spans.output:
[{"role":"assistant","parts":[{"type":"text","content":"Sounds gucci. I reran the full March export with overwrite using [scripts/export-tempo-traces.py](scripts/export-tempo-traces.py), after fixing the fallback window logic to search both local-day and UTC-day windows and to write both views into the metadata. The final coverage is unchanged: `28 available`, `1 partial`, `31 missing`.

The timezone point is handled now. For example, [data/analysis/304139/gpt-5.4-2026-04-11/trace-metadata.json](data/analysis/304139/gpt-5.4-2026-04-11/trace-metadata.json) shows:
- `windowLocal`: `2026-04-10T21:45:00-07:00` to `2026-04-10T21:55:55-07:00`

spans.input: [{"role":"user","parts":[{"type":"text","content":"> 304139/gpt-5.4-2026-04-11: missing (0 trace(s), 0 span(s))\n\nwhy do we only look at 04-11? I started the task yesterday 04-10 (be careful with timezone)"}]}]
spans.output:
[{"role":"assistant","parts":[{"type":"text","content":"Sounds gucci. The `04-11` in `304139/gpt-5.4-2026-04-11` is just the experiment directory label, not the only day the exporter should search. The real bug was in [scripts/export-tempo-traces.py](scripts/export-tempo-traces.py): its fallback search used a single UTC calendar day derived from the run-window start, so the fallback logic hid the local `04-10` context.

I fixed that. The exporter now searches fallback windows for both the local day and the UTC day, across every day the run window spans, and it records both views in [data/analysis/304139/gpt-5.4-2026-04-11/trace-metadata.json](data/analysis/304139/gpt-5.4-2026-04-11/trace-metadata.json). For `304139`, the exact run window is now shown as:
- local: `2026-04-10T21:45:00-07:00` to `2026-04-10T21:55:55-07:00`
- UTC: `2026-04-11T04:45:00Z` to `2026-04-11T04:55:55Z`

I reran the exporter for `304139` after the fix, and it is still `missing`. That means this one is not being missed because we only searched `04-11`; it is missing even after searching the exact local window plus both fallback day windows.

Next step I recommend:
1. Rerun the full March export with the corrected window logic and regenerate the trace index.
2. Then we can hide or mark only the analyses that are still genuinely missing from Tempo.
3. If you want strict coverage, we can rerun just the missing analyses to regenerate both outputs and traces."}]}]
